text.title: Diary Study Overview
text.summary: What diary studies are, the three study types, when to use and when not to use, and key design decisions.

section.title: Definition
section.p: A diary study is a longitudinal research method where participants record experiences, behaviors, and thoughts in the moment or shortly after, over an extended period. It captures real-world context that lab studies erase and reveals how behavior evolves over time.

rule.statement: Choose the study type based on what triggers a relevant event for your research question.
rule.rationale: Each type captures different behavioral signals. Mixing types within one study produces incommensurable data.

list.item: Interval-contingent: entries at fixed times (daily, weekly) — for routine behaviors and scheduled usage.
Event-contingent: entries when a specific event occurs — for specific interactions and triggered behaviors.
Signal-contingent: entries on random prompts — for capturing natural moments without scheduling bias.

rule.statement: Use diary studies when the behavior spans multiple days and context matters. Do not use them when you need findings within two weeks or for rare events.
rule.rationale: Minimum useful diary period is 7-14 days. Studies of rare events produce too few entries per participant to identify patterns.

list.item: Good for: understanding context, behavior over time, habit formation, experience evolution, real-world patterns.
Not good for: quick insights, immediate feedback, rare events, usability details, controlled comparisons.

rule.statement: Cap study duration at 10-21 days for most products; 28+ days only for true habit-formation research.
rule.rationale: Participant fatigue causes 30-50% entry dropout after day 10 in studies without strong re-engagement. Studies longer than 3 weeks lose late entries to "same as yesterday" responses.

rule.statement: Each diary entry must take under 3 minutes to complete. If the form is longer, split into a quick log and a weekly deep-dive.
rule.rationale: Entry burden is the top predictor of dropout. Participants with demanding forms disengage before the study reaches the habit-formation window.

list.item: Duration: days, weeks, or months depending on the behavior under study.
Frequency: daily, event-based, or random signal.
Format: dedicated app, SMS, email, paper — choose based on participant tech comfort.
Entry format: text, photo, video, rating — mix modalities for richer corpora.
Questions: structured (faster to complete) or open (richer but harder to synthesize).
